data.title: Badger Meter anticipates 2026 organic revenue in line with 2025 as it agrees to acquire UDlive for $100M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Badger Meter (BMI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "First quarter sales were down 9% year-over-year to $202 million," as "short-cycle order rates... were weaker than we anticipated, resulting in approximately $15 million to $20 million of lower revenue versus our internal expectations" (Chairman, President & CEO Kenneth Bockhorst).
  • "We previously communicated that our 2026 revenues would be weighted toward the back half of the year" and "our expectations for a solid second half have not changed," while "the softer start up to the year prompts us to anticipate full year 2026 organic revenue to be on balance with 2025" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • "This project set represents between 2.6 million and 3.6 million connections over multiple years" and "we view the first half of 2026 as a short pause, not a break in our trajectory" (Executive Vice President of North America Municipal Utility Robert Wrocklage).
  • "We've implemented measured cost reduction actions including a 10% salary reduction for our executive officers for the next 6 months to maintain spending discipline and protect margin integrity" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • "Total sales were $202 million," "gross margin was 41.7%," "operating earnings were approximately $35.2 million and operating margin was 17.4%," and "diluted earnings per share were $0.93" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Daniel Weltzien).

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect 2026 activity to be back-half weighted as awarded AMI projects advance into deployment" and "we expect sequential improvement in absolute quarterly revenue dollars as the year progresses" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • "We expect second quarter 2026 organic revenue dollars to sequentially improve from the trough of Q1" but "expect them to be down year-over-year against the highest quarterly revenue figure in the company's history" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing that "we expect this project pacing dynamic to extend throughout the first half of 2026," management in Q1 added that the Q1 short-cycle softness "is timing related and does not reflect a structural change" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Analyst estimates were provided in a separate JSON tag, but its fields did not match the required format described in the instructions (for example, quarter values were numeric rather than Q1–Q4), so no estimates comparison is included.

Financial Results

  • "Total sales were $202 million, representing a 9% decline year-over-year"; "utility water sales declined 10%"; and "sales for the flow instrumentation product line were down 4% year-over-year" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • "Lower metering product revenue was partially offset by increased BEACON SaaS, smart cover, water quality and network monitoring product revenues" and "collectively, beyond the meter product line growth was a bright spot in the quarter" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • "Selling, engineering and administrative expenses were $49.2 million" including "$1.2 million in transaction costs associated with the UDlive acquisition"; "the effective income tax rate was 24.8%"; and "we generated strong free cash flow in the quarter of about $30 million" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • "In the first quarter of 2026, we repurchased 256,000 shares for a total of $38 million and have $115 million left on our share repurchase authorization" (VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).

Q&A

  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Asked if the $15 million to $20 million short-cycle shortfall was weather-related or something else; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst answered, "there's really not one underlying thing" and called it "relatively normal," while Executive Vice President Wrocklage added, "we're not chalking it up to geographic weather" and said "this is absolutely timing-based."
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Pressed on PRASA timing and reliability; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said, "the confidence is higher today than it was before" citing "the fact that we have a PO" and that "installation partners are lined up."
  • Jeffrey Reive, RBC Capital Markets: Asked the risk that late starts push into 2027 and whether the outlook is conservative; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said, "our confidence level is better today than it was 90 days ago" as projects move toward deployment.
  • Quinn Fredrickson, Baird: Asked whether the flat organic outlook assumes a short-cycle recovery; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said, "we do expect some upside... compared to the current quarter," while Executive Vice President Wrocklage cautioned, "realize what we're saying for the whole year is flat-ish."
  • Andrew Krill, Deutsche Bank: Asked about gross margin trajectory and then Section 232 tariffs; Executive Vice President Wrocklage said, "the 39% to 42% range, we still have confidence in" and on tariffs, "our tariff exposure... hasn't really changed," adding, "I don't think about tariffs differently than I have over the last couple of quarters."
  • Scott Graham, Seaport: Asked about incremental margins and whether PRASA hurts; Executive Vice President Wrocklage said PRASA gross margins are "not quite at the line average level" but "the SEA leverage... gets us back to an OP leverage that's in line with the rest of the business," and added, "our SCA dollars have been relatively flat."
  • William Grippin, Barclays: Asked about sewer monitoring market penetration and payback; Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst said adoption is "less than 0.5% of the manhole covers in the U.S." and "there's almost an immediate payback on cleaning optimization."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing, repeatedly probing timing risk and visibility (for example, "what's the risk that some of the late to starts push into 2027?" and "does that like post downside risk to the guidance?").
  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly negative on the quarter but confidence-oriented on timing ("this first quarter outcome is timing related") and became more defensive in Q&A on specifics ("we're not going to comment on price project to project") (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more upbeat record-performance framing ("record sales, profitability and cash flow"), the current call used more variability language ("air pocket" and "uneven") while reiterating long-term positioning and project funnel visibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 emphasized "record sales, profitability and cash flow" and described project pacing as having "very little to do" with demand or budgets; Q1 emphasized a sales decline tied to both project pacing and short-cycle softness, with management saying it is "timing related" (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst; VP, CFO & Treasurer Weltzien).
  • Q4 highlighted PRASA as "approximately 1.6 million service connections" with shipments expected in 2026; Q1 added "the first significant purchase order" and said deployment partners are expected to begin activity "around midyear" (Executive Vice President Wrocklage).
  • Q1 introduced new capital allocation and cost actions not discussed in Q4 detail, including "a 10% salary reduction for our executive officers for the next 6 months" and a signed agreement to acquire UDlive.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Short-cycle order rates... were weaker than we anticipated" and management said there is "the least amount of visibility" in that part of the business (Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst).
  • Management repeatedly highlighted timing variability in deployments, with Chairman, President & CEO Bockhorst noting that over a multi-year project "over a 3-month period, it's really not" predictable, and Executive Vice President Wrocklage citing PRASA-specific disruption risk: "it's to hurricanes."
  • On tariffs, Executive Vice President Wrocklage said exposure "hasn't really changed" but added it is "always subject to change" as of April 17.

Final Takeaway

Badger Meter management framed Q1’s sales decline as the intersection of AMI project pacing and a short-cycle “air pocket,” and said it now expects 2026 organic revenue (excluding UDlive) to be in line with 2025 while still “back-half weighted.” Executives pointed to awarded AMI projects totaling “between 2.6 million and 3.6 million connections,” disclosed a $100 million agreement to acquire U.K.-based UDlive (expected to be EPS accretive in year 1), and described near-term cost discipline actions—including a 10% executive salary reduction for six months—while reiterating that the Q1 weakness was “timing-based” rather than structural.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top rated ETFs with exposure to Alcoa, as company reports Q1 results
data.description:
Alcoa (AA) fell over 6% in Friday's session after its Q1 adjusted EPS of $1.40 missed estimates by $0.15 and revenue of $3.19 billion came in $80 million below consensus, a 5% decline year-over-year, as lower alumina shipments and pricing weighed on results.

In light of this, below is a list of 12 ETFs with exposure to Alcoa, ranked according to their Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings. The list evaluates funds based on quantitative metrics to help identify potential investment opportunities.

The list is topped by State Street SPDR S&P Metals & Mining ETF (XME), with a Quant Rating of 4.39. MarketDesk Focused US Momentum ETF (FMTM) and First Trust Materials AlphaDEX Fund ETF (FXZ) follow closely behind, each earning Buy ratings. First Trust Active Factor Mid Cap ETF (AFMC) rounds out the top four with a rating of 3.77.

The funds on this list represent a diverse range of strategies, from basic materials and momentum-focused ETFs to various mid-cap value index funds from providers like Vanguard, iShares and State Street. Ratings on this list range from a high of 4.39 to a low of 2.80, with the Cambria Value and Momentum ETF (VAMO) at the bottom of the rankings.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system grades funds on their relative performance across critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum and profitability. Ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered a bullish rating and any rating of 2.5 or below considered a bearish rating.

Here is the list:

  1. State Street SPDR S&P Metals & Mining ETF (XME), Quant Rating: 4.39

  2. MarketDesk Focused US Momentum ETF (FMTM), Quant Rating: 4.30

  3. First Trust Materials AlphaDEX Fund ETF (FXZ), Quant Rating: 4.14

  4. First Trust Active Factor Mid Cap ETF (AFMC), Quant Rating: 3.77

  5. Invesco Dorsey Wright Basic Materials Momentum ETF (PYZ), Quant Rating: 3.38

  6. Strategas Macro Momentum ETF (SAMM), Quant Rating: 3.24

  7. Euclidean Fundamental Value ETF (ECML), Quant Rating: 3.22

  8. Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value Index Fund ETF (IVOV), Quant Rating: 3.21

  9. iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF (IJJ), Quant Rating: 3.20

  10. State Street SPDR S&P 400 Mid Cap Value ETF (MDYV), Quant Rating: 3.19

  11. First Trust Mid Cap Value AlphaDEX Fund ETF (FNK), Quant Rating: 3.03

  12. Cambria Value and Momentum ETF (VAMO), Quant Rating: 2.80

data.title: Weight loss drug developer Kailera climbs 50% on trading debut
data.description:
Shares of Kailera Therapeutics (KLRA) jumped more than 50% on Friday after the GLP-1 drug developer priced its upsized initial public offering at $16 per share to raise roughly $625M in gross proceeds.

Shares of the Waltham, Massachusetts-based biotech opened for trading at $26 per share at about 1:20 PM ET, implying a gain of about 63%. The stock quickly retraced initial gains to reach $24.90 about 30 minutes later, indicating a rise of nearly 56% and a market capitalization of approximately $3.1B.

Earlier in the day, the company priced the offering of approximately 39.0M shares of its common stock, upsized from its initial plans to sell roughly 33.3M shares.

Founded in 2024, Kailera has four GLP-1 candidates in the pipeline. Its lead asset, a GLP-1/GIP receptor dual agonist known as ribupatide, developed with Chinese pharma Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals (JHPCY) (JNGHF), is undergoing three late-stage clinical trials for weight management, with top-line data expected in 2028.

data.title: U.S. stocks rise to new highs as the Strait of Hormuz is declared open
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages jumped on Friday to record highs after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels. President Donald Trump, however, said the naval blockade will remain in effect.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +1.2%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.5%, and the Dow (DJI) was +2%.

The Ports and Maritime Organization of the Islamic Republic of Iran said the Strait of Hormuz is now “completely open” for all commercial vessels for the rest of the Lebanon ceasefire timeline. This comes after Israel and Lebanon announced a 10-day ceasefire on Thursday.

Nonetheless, Trump said that the naval blockade on Iranian ports will remain in full force and effect. The reopening of the passageway was one of the conditions of the fragile two-week ceasefire that is currently in effect.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) rapidly decreased to $83, while Brent (CO1:COM) also dropped to $90 per barrel.

In addition, the U.S. dollar (DXY) retreated sharply, -0.2%, erasing its recent Middle East conflict-driven gains as the Strait of Hormuz was declared open to commercial ships by Iran.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 7 basis points lower at 4.25%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 8 basis points to 3.71%.

“The reported reopening of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran is undoubtedly a welcome relief for the global economy. Needless to say, its true impact depends on the durability of this reopening, which will require sustained confidence-building measures from all three warring parties,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

“Equally critical is the reaction of ship operators. While their immediate priority is to bring out vessels currently in the region as soon as they can, the real litmus test lies in their willingness to send new ships in. Their return is necessary for restoring the global energy flow and bringing regional production sites fully back online,” El-Erian added.

data.title: Merck gains after six-day losing streak
data.description:
Merck (NYSE: MRK) rose after six straight sessions of losses, with shares up 2.17% at $117.97 on Friday afternoon.

Over the prior six sessions, the stock had fallen 6.27%, underperforming a 3.81% gain in the S&P 500 Index. However, the shares are up 11.05% in 2026, compared with a 2.86% rise in the broader index.

According to Seeking Alpha analyst Edmund Ingham, Merck’s agreement to acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals for $53 per share, valuing the deal at about $6.7 billion, had been viewed as a move to strengthen its oncology pipeline, with the analyst maintaining a Buy rating on the stock.

 He had noted that early-stage data for TERN-701 had shown strong response rates, outperforming existing therapies such as Novartis’ Scemblix. The deal had been considered part of Merck’s strategy to offset the upcoming Keytruda patent expiry, with TERN-701 positioned as a potential growth driver, while execution and regulatory risks had remained.

Separately, in a recent update, the European Commission had approved Merck’s Keytruda-based combination therapy with chemotherapy (paclitaxel) for certain adults with ovarian cancer, expanding its use as a late-line treatment option in platinum-resistant disease. The decision had followed earlier endorsement from the European Medicines Agency, which had supported the marketing application for the indication last month.

Meanwhile, according to Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, MRK held a Hold rating with a score of 3.26 out of 5, with the stock rated A+ for profitability, D for growth, and B+ for momentum.

According to Wall Street analysts, 19 rated the stock a Buy or above, 10 rated it a Hold, while none rated it a Sell or lower.

Seeking Alpha analysts also remained broadly positive on the stock, maintaining a Buy call for MRK.

data.title: WaFd targets 8%-12% active loan growth while pursuing 20% noninterest deposits by 2030
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WaFd, Inc. (WAFD) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “No question, the headline for this quarter is loan growth,” said (President, CEO & Vice Chairman Brent Beardall), highlighting that “this quarter, we saw growth in the overall net loan portfolio, including inactive segments,” and adding, “we saw a 12% increase on a linked-quarter basis in the active portfolio, and if you include, the yet to be funded loans, gross active loans outstanding increased by 20% on a linked-quarter basis.”

  • (Executive VP & CFO Kelli Holz) reported, “WaFd, Inc. reported net income available to common shareholders of $61.9 million or $0.82 per diluted share for the quarter ended March 31, 2026,” and tied the linked-quarter EPS increase to “a modest increase in net interest income, controlled expenses as well as 2.7 million shares repurchased during the quarter at a weighted average price of $31.85 per share or 1.05x tangible book value.”

  • (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Ryan Mauer) said the company delivered “a solid quarter of new loan production,” adding, “Importantly, we were able to achieve this level of loan production with a consistent approach to underwriting that maintained a moderate risk profile,” while also noting credit trends improved: “Nonperforming assets decreased to $132 million or 0.48% of total assets from $203 million or 0.75% at December 31, 2025.”

  • (CEO Beardall) framed Build 2030 as a strategic reorientation: “Our strategic plan, Build 2030 is designed to fully shift our focus to where we can add the most value for our clients and shareholders, serving the banking needs of businesses,” with “the most important goal” being “increasing our noninterest-bearing deposits to total deposits from 11% last year up to 20% by 2030,” and “Today, we sit at 12.2%.”

Outlook

  • (CFO Holz) said, “Absent any changes in interest rates, we expect our margin to be flat in the near-term,” and pointed to a future earnings tailwind: “One piece of good news that will materialize going forward for us is the accretion of $167 million of deferred income related to the interest rate mark on the Luther Burbank loan portfolio. Currently, this is being accreted into income at a rate of $6 million per quarter. We expect this to accelerate as these loans begin to adjust or repay.”

  • (CEO Beardall) reiterated a profitability target and its building blocks: “If we can get our margin up just a little bit higher to 3%, which is our short-term goal over the next 2-years,” then “everything else being equal, our ROTCE would be 12.5%,” adding, “The key from my perspective is growth in direct C&I loans and low-cost deposits.”

  • (CEO Beardall) outlined growth expectations and pipeline commentary: “We believe that we have the products and the teams in place to grow our active loan portfolios by 8% to 12% going forward,” while adding that the lending pipeline “decreased from $3.6 billion as of December to $3.2 billion” due to “robust originations,” and the deposit pipeline “increased from $264 million as of December 31 to $439 million as of March 31.”

Financial results

  • (CFO Holz) said loans increased: “Loans receivable increased $119 million during the quarter,” while deposits fell: “Total deposits decreased by $292 million,” and borrowings rose: “borrowings, which increased $626 million.”

  • (CFO Holz) reported profitability drivers in spread income: “Net interest income increased $6.5 million from the prior quarter,” and “The net interest margin was 2.81% in the March quarter compared to 2.7% for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.”

  • (CFO Holz) described fee and expense items: “Total noninterest income decreased $400,000 compared to the prior quarter to $19.8 million,” with “$6.7 million in commission revenue from our WaFd Insurance subsidiary,” while “Total noninterest expense increased $4.1 million or 3.9% from the prior quarter,” and “The company's efficiency ratio for the quarter was 55.7% compared to 55.3% in the prior quarter.”

  • (CFO Holz) updated capital and liquidity: “We estimate our CET1 ratio at quarter end to be 11.4% and our total risk-based capital ratio to be 14.4%,” and “Liquidity is strong with $4.2 billion of on-balance sheet liquidity.”

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: “you mentioned 7 basis points on nonaccrual improvement. Is that the linked quarter swing?” (Executive VP & CFO Holz): “So that's the linked-quarter swing... there was about $2.2 million that was recognized this quarter, that is really from prior quarter activities.”

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about the path to a 3% margin and whether securities growth headwinds were “baked into the expectation.” (CEO Beardall): “We think just organically, as we reprice these mortgage loans and loans come due, we have that accretion to come into income,” and on securities, “We'll probably take our foot of the gas on that as well going forward. So there won't be additional headwinds from that.”

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked whether C&I growth came from “[SYMET's] or club deals.” (CEO Beardall): “I don't think any of that growth came from SMYET's or club deals at this point... these are direct originations,” and (CFO Holz) added, “I can get that number and get back to that.”

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about the durability of fee income near “$20 million.” (CEO Beardall): “No, I think that's sustainable,” while also cautioning, “Right now, wealth, we're still working to get profitable... we're not planning on... selling the SBA portion.”

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Pressed on deposit attachment to new commercial relationships and expense run-rate. (CEO Beardall): “every one of these commercial relationships come with core operating accounts,” and on expenses, “this is the quarter where we see the annual merit increases kick-in,” concluding, “this is a good run rate for now.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive and detail-oriented, repeatedly pressing on the mechanics of margin improvement and funding mix (e.g., Kelly Motta, KBW: “Squaring your margin expectations to get to 3% over time, clearly requires some -- bringing on some low-cost funding”).

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and pragmatic in Q&A, emphasizing execution and near-term constraints while asserting confidence (CEO Beardall: “No question, the headline for this quarter is loan growth”; CFO Holz: “Absent any changes in interest rates, we expect our margin to be flat in the near-term”).

  • Versus last quarter’s heavier focus on nonaccrual-driven margin pressure, management emphasized improved momentum and execution this quarter (CEO Beardall: “we are making significant progress to becoming a business bank”).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call shifted emphasis from prior-quarter credit and margin headwinds to growth traction, with management highlighting that “this quarter, we saw growth in the overall net loan portfolio” after “over a year of seeing our loan portfolio contract.”

  • Margin language changed from Q1’s expectation of “more pressure on the margin from additional mortgage-backed securities purchases” (CFO Holz, Q1) to Q2’s “we expect our margin to be flat in the near-term” (CFO Holz, Q2), alongside CEO commentary that securities buying will slow.

  • Credit discussion improved sequentially as NPAs fell in Q2 after rising in Q1, while management continued to emphasize policy discipline and borrower engagement (CCO Mauer, Q2: “we are actively collaborating with the borrower to resolve the issues”).

  • Analysts’ focus rotated from Q1’s questions on nonaccruals, borrowings repricing, and expense growth to Q2’s questions on margin normalization, deposit capture from commercial growth, and the sustainability of fee income.

Risks and concerns

  • (CCO Mauer) said credit remains exposed to macro headwinds: “the elevated interest rate environment has impacted borrowers' expense structures,” and “economic uncertainty originally driven by tariffs with further expected impact by war in the Middle East and energy supply shocks will continue to impact borrowers' top line revenue results as well as operating costs.”

  • (CCO Mauer) flagged concentration in delinquency/NPA metrics: “delinquencies and nonperforming assets are impacted by a large commercial relationship over 90-days past due... $51 million,” adding, “there was no charge-off taken upon revaluation at this point.”

  • (CEO Beardall) highlighted intensifying deposit competition, including a new entrant: “it looks to be getting even more challenging with the upcoming entrance of Elon Musk into the space with his new product, X Money,” citing “a 6% rate on FDIC-insured deposits and 3% cash-back on debit card purchases,” and adding, “it certainly has our full attention.”

Final takeaway

Management positioned Q2 fiscal 2026 as an execution quarter for Build 2030, emphasizing a return to net loan growth, a still-competitive deposit environment, and a near-term expectation for a flat margin absent rate changes while working toward a 3% margin goal over two years. Leadership highlighted capital flexibility through buybacks and potential regulatory capital relief, while maintaining that credit metrics improved but remain sensitive to a $51 million large relationship and broader macro uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: When will Hormuz shipping normalize? See what traders are predicting
data.description:
Wall Street extended gains Friday afternoon as easing tensions in the Middle East improved investor sentiment, though uncertainty around key shipping routes remained.

Iranian authorities announced that the Strait of Hormuz is fully open to commercial traffic for the duration of the Lebanon ceasefire, a move aimed at stabilizing global trade flows. 

Despite the announcement, President Donald Trump said U.S. naval restrictions on Iranian ports will remain in place, creating a mixed outlook for maritime activity in the region. The conflicting signals have left traders focused on how quickly shipping conditions can normalize.

Prediction market participants are increasingly weighing the timeline for a full recovery in transit through the critical waterway, reflecting cautious optimism alongside lingering geopolitical risk. See below what traders on prediction market Kalshi are saying: 

  • Before May 1, 2026 — 46% chance.
  • Before May 15, 2026 — 64% chance.
  • Before Jun 1, 2026 — 78% chance.
  • Before Jul 1, 2026 — 84% chance. 
  • Before Aug 1, 2026 — 88% chance.
  • Before Sep 1, 2026 — 86% chance.
  • Before Oct 1, 2026 — 91% chance.
  • Before Jan 1, 2027 — 93.9% chance. 
  • Before Apr 1, 2027 — 95% chance. 
  • Before Jul 1, 2027 — 97% chance. 

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL). 

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL). 

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Patrick Industries, rival LCI in talks to combine - Bloomberg
data.description:
Patrick Industries (PATK) is in discussions to combine with rival recreational-vehicle supplier LCI Industries (LCII).

The companies are negotiating a potential all-stock deal, according to a  Bloomberg report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. No final decisions have been made and discussions could fall apart.

LCI declined to comment to Bloomberg, while Patrick Industries didn’t immediately provide comment.

Shares of LCI Industries rose 3.9%, while Patrick gained rose 5.4%,

Patrick (PATK) is set to report Q1 results on April 30.

data.title: F.N.B. projects $1.495B-$1.535B 2026 net interest income while assuming no Fed cuts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: F.N.B. Corporation (FNB) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "F.N.B. produced a solid quarter with net income of $137 million" and "EPS increased 19% over the first quarter of 2025 to $0.38," said (Chairman, President & CEO Vincent J. Delie), adding that "period-end loan growth of 3.9% annualized linked quarter was driven by core middle market C&I" and that growth "has not benefited from NDFI or lending into private credit, a category that we continue to avoid."
  • "We recently announced an 8% increase to our quarterly cash dividend to $0.13 per share" and "an additional $250 million for the repurchase of our common stock on top of the $50 million remaining," said (CEO Delie), also noting "$35 million repurchased in the first quarter of 2026" and that "F.N.B. has returned a total of $2.4 billion in capital to shareholders through both dividends and repurchases since 2009."
  • "We were thrilled to recently announce our partnership as the official and exclusive retail bank and financial provider to the Pennsylvania State University," said (CEO Delie), adding, "Beginning in July, Penn State's 90,000 students, faculty and staff will have exclusive access to F.N.B.'s on-campus banking services, including our proprietary eStore" and that F.N.B. "was also selected as the primary treasury management provider to all Penn State campuses."
  • "This month, our first ATM that offers foreign currency disbursement for Canadian dollars and Mexican pesos opened at the new Pittsburgh International Airport," said (CEO Delie), calling it "very rare across the banking industry."
  • "First quarter net income totaled $137 million or $0.38 per share, with total revenues up a strong 9.4% from a year ago period," said (Chief Financial Officer Vincent J. Calabrese), and added that "first quarter's net interest margin was 3.25%, down 3 basis points sequentially" and "the margin for the month of March was at 3.30%."

Outlook

  • "We are maintaining our full year balance sheet guidance for spot balances, projecting period-end loans and deposits to grow mid-single digits on a full year basis," said (CFO Calabrese).
  • "Full year net interest income is still expected to be between $1.495 billion and $1.535 billion," said (CFO Calabrese), while changing the rate assumption: "We are assuming no Fed interest rate cuts for 2026 versus our previous expectation for 2 25 basis point cuts."
  • "Second quarter net interest income is projected between $370 million and $380 million," said (CFO Calabrese), and "second quarter levels" of noninterest income are expected "between $90 million and $95 million."
  • "The full year guidance range for noninterest expense remains unchanged between $1 billion and $1.02 billion, but we now expect to be at the higher end of that range due to increased investments in franchise growth and new strategic initiatives," said (CFO Calabrese).

Financial results

  • "Loan activity began to accelerate late in the quarter" and "spot total loans and leases" ended at "$35.1 billion," said (CFO Calabrese), with "spot total deposits" at "$38.9 billion" and "noninterest-bearing deposits" up "$89 million."
  • "Noninterest income totaled $91 million," said (CFO Calabrese), including "capital markets income" of "$6.8 million" and "Wealth Management revenues" of "$21.8 million."
  • "Noninterest expense totaled $257.9 million," said (CFO Calabrese), and "other noninterest expense increased $6.8 million or 30% due to a combination of higher fraud losses, litigation-related expenses, and the impact of our mortgage down payment assistance program."
  • "The first quarter efficiency ratio remained solid at 56.1%," said (CFO Calabrese), and capital levels were framed as "the TCE ratio of nearly 9% and the CET1 ratio of 11.4%."

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about C&I acceleration and pipelines; (Chief Credit Officer Gary L. Guerrieri) said, "The pipelines have increased significantly and are pretty close to near record levels" and cited "high-quality opportunities" and "some M&A activity."
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked what drove higher-end expense guidance; (CFO Calabrese) said, "There's a variety," including "Clicks-to-Bricks," "30 de novos over the next 5 years," and investments to "create a 360 view of our customers."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Asked about NIM and deposit costs; (CFO Calabrese) said the March exit margin was "3.30%" and that guidance "implies that going up gradually a few basis points or so a quarter" as deposit seasonality and reinvestment/roll-offs evolve.
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: Asked about capital posture and Basel III; (CFO Calabrese) said, "we continue to see buybacks attractive at current valuation levels," and on Basel III, "it has a meaningful impact if it gets in place the way it is" and "that's not baked into our plans here."
  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Pressed on deposit pricing pressure; (CFO Calabrese) said "there's still opportunity for that cost of deposits to come down" while (CEO Delie) said the key lever is "increasing the noninterest-bearing component in the mix."
  • David Smith, Truist: Asked about keeping mid-single-digit loan growth without expected cuts; (CEO Delie) pointed to pipelines, saying commercial pipelines "are up 10%" and that consumer pipelines were "nearly at an all-time high."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about M&A appetite; (CEO Delie) said, "we're going to be opportunistic" and "We're not actively in the market."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on funding and margin sustainability, including "deposit pricing pressure" (Gunther) and "NIM outlook" (Haire).
  • Management tone slightly positive in prepared remarks, emphasizing capital return and growth, including (CEO Delie) "We recently announced an 8% increase" and (CFO Calabrese) "We continue to expect strong positive operating leverage."
  • Compared with last quarter, the guidance language shifted on rates from (CFO Calabrese, Q4) "Our guidance assumes 2 25 basis point rate cuts" to (CFO Calabrese, Q1) "We are assuming no Fed interest rate cuts for 2026," while maintaining the net interest income range.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Capital return actions were newly emphasized in Q1, including (CEO Delie) "an additional $250 million" buyback authorization and "an 8% increase" in the dividend; in Q4, (CFO Calabrese) said for buybacks, "for 2026, I would expect we'd be at the same level or higher" and dividend discussions were described as "on the table."
  • Loan growth commentary moved from Q4 balance-sheet “reset” to Q1 acceleration, with (CEO Delie, Q4) "we focused on resetting the balance sheet" and (CFO Calabrese, Q1) "Loan activity began to accelerate late in the quarter."
  • Expense messaging shifted from Q4 expectations of cost takeouts to Q1’s near-term reinvestment, with (CFO Calabrese, Q1) expecting to be "at the higher end" of the expense range due to "new strategic initiatives."

Risks and concerns

  • "Delinquency, along with NPLs and OREO increased slightly," said (Chief Credit Officer Guerrieri), while "criticized loans increased slightly, consistent with the seasonality" and provision expense was "$19.4 million" to support growth.
  • "While we have not experienced any impact related to tariffs, we are maintaining the related qualitative overlays from a year ago due to the ongoing conflict and uncertainty in the Middle East," said (CCO Guerrieri), adding that the company is "monitoring the situation" closely.
  • Expense headwinds included "higher fraud losses" and "litigation-related expenses," per (CFO Calabrese), with mitigation framed as "disciplined" expense management and investments intended to "extract costs as we move forward."

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a quarter of accelerating loan activity and stable credit metrics alongside a step-up in capital return, highlighted by a dividend increase, expanded buyback authorization, and maintained full-year guidance ranges despite changing the interest-rate outlook to no Fed cuts in 2026, while signaling higher-end expense expectations tied to technology, data, and growth initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Autoliv reiterates 2026 adjusted operating margin of 10.5% to 11% and $1.2B operating cash flow, while flagging $90M raw material headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Autoliv, Inc. (ALV) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter exceeded our expectations, driven by strong sales in March" (President, CEO Mikael Bratt), alongside "solid productivity improvements" and "reduced call-off volatility" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Our positive trend in Asia continued with strong growth in India, South Korea and China" (President, CEO Bratt). He added, "In China, we continued to grow faster than light vehicle production especially with the Chinese OEMs, outperforming by more than 40 percentage points" (President, CEO Bratt), and said, "In India, we grew sales by 38% organically" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Buybacks were paused as the company was in a restricted period following multiple filings and the announcement of a new CFO" (President, CEO Bratt). He reiterated that "Our USD 2.5 billion share repurchase authorization through 2029 remains unchanged" and stated "the ambition annual share repurchase between USD 300 million and USD 500 million" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Hostilities in the Persian Gulf had a limited impact this quarter" (President, CEO Bratt), and he said, "Based on what we know today, we reiterate our full year 2026 guidance of flat organic sales" and "an adjusted operating margin of around 10.5% to 11%" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "I am also pleased that we introduced our first air bag for motorcycles as well as our first complete wearable airbag solution" (President, CEO Bratt), describing it as "building on our long-term strategy of growing outside our traditional core business" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Our net sales were almost $2.8 billion, representing a 7% increase" and "The adjusted operating income decreased from USD 255 million to USD 245 million" (Executive VP of Finance & CFO Monika Grama). She also said, "During the quarter, we recovered approximately 70% of our U.S. tariff costs" (CFO Grama).

Outlook

  • "We expect to outperform light vehicle production by around 1 percentage points" and reiterated "Our organic sales is expected to be flat" (President, CEO Bratt), with guidance assumptions including "no material changes to tariffs or trade restrictions that are in effect as of April 10, 2026" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "For the full year 2026, our current assessment is for around USD 90 million gross impact from higher raw material pricing compared the previous assessment of around $30 million a quarter ago" (President, CEO Bratt). He added, "customer compensation mechanisms are in place and are expected to offset a meaningful portion of the cost impact, although there is typically a timing delay" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "Operating cash flow is expected to be around USD 1.2 billion" and "We expect CapEx to be below 5% of sales" (President, CEO Bratt), while also stating "we expect a tax rate of around 28%" (President, CEO Bratt).

Financial results

  • "Our consolidated net sales were almost USD 2.8 billion, the highest for a first quarter yet" (President, CEO Bratt). He said the increase was "mainly driven by USD 154 million positive currency translation effect and USD 14 million from higher tariff-related compensation" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • "The adjusted operating margin was 8.9%, 1 percentage points lower than in the same quarter last year" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama said, "The reported operating income of USD 237 million was USD 8 million lower mainly due to capacity alignment activities" (CFO Grama).

  • "Operating cash flow was a negative USD 76 million" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama attributed the year-over-year change primarily to "a negative working effect of USD 349 million" and said the working-capital impact was "mainly due to temporary impact" (CFO Grama).

  • "The combination of unrecovered tariffs and the dilutive effect of the recovered portion resulted in a negative impact of around 40 basis points on our operating margin in the quarter" (CFO Grama), and she said, "We expect, though, most of the outstanding tariffs to be recovered later in the year" (CFO Grama).

Q&A

  • Gautam Narayan, RBC: asked whether mix between domestic vs. nondomestic OEMs in China benefited margins and how sustainable it is; President, CEO Bratt responded, "we don't disclose a breakdown of our earnings profile for customers or regions" and said, "we have focused on our Chinese OEMs as they are growing in their share of the total market" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Gautam Narayan, RBC: asked about Section 232 and USMCA exemptions; President, CEO Bratt said, "for us, it's mainly the USMCA structure that is relevant and that we have no changes at this point" and that recent rule changes were "a minor part of our total exposure and not meaningful" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: questioned why Autoliv’s planning assumption differs from S&P’s 2026 LVP outlook and asked about downside sensitivity; President, CEO Bratt said changes are "within the... margin of error" and added, "we have a high degree of flexibility in our system" while citing "normal decrementals... between 20% and 30% leverage" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Colin Langan, Wells Fargo: asked about drivers of higher raw-material costs and potential nylon shortages; President, CEO Bratt said "it's really the oil price that is the main driver" and added on supply, "we don't really see at this point any main concerns" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Mattias Holmberg, DNB Carnegie: asked why Q1 outperformance doesn’t carry through full-year guidance and about phasing/recovery of the $90 million raw-material headwind; President, CEO Bratt cited "mix development throughout the year" and said the net effect "is included in our guidance" while adding, "we will have a majority through the price mechanisms" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Hampus Engellau, Handelsbanken: asked if call-off stability is sustainable and about China launch timing; President, CEO Bratt said "the call-off stability was around 95%" and "we still believe that it should continue to improve under normal circumstances" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama added on launches, "I wouldn't say that we have any surprises" (CFO Grama).

  • Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe: asked for mix assumptions and margin expansion drivers; President, CEO Bratt said full-year planning assumes "a neutral mix" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama said the company plans to offset "negative impact from raw materials and from inflationary impact on SG&A and RD&E" with "structural cost savings" and "customer compensations" (CFO Grama).

  • Jose Asumendi, JPMorgan: asked about benefiting from Chinese OEMs in Europe and working capital; President, CEO Bratt said "we are present in a broad base" of Chinese OEM platforms and referenced "2 exceptions" with captive solutions (President, CEO Bratt), while adding, "we won the first tender that was issued in Europe by Chinese OEM" (President, CEO Bratt). CFO Grama said full-year expectations imply "normalizing the working capital assumptions" (CFO Grama).

  • Jairam Nathan, Daiwa: asked whether the motorcycle airbag changes long-term growth assumptions and about buybacks; President, CEO Bratt said, "it doesn't really change the expectation" and that Mobility Safety Solutions contribution becomes meaningful "2030 before it becomes meaningful" (President, CEO Bratt), and added on repurchases, "We remain committed" (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Björn Enarson, Danske Bank: asked about neutral mix assumptions vs. prior-year headwinds and Middle East exposure; President, CEO Bratt said, "we had some headwind last year" and "We are not expecting that to be reversed this year" (President, CEO Bratt), and added Middle East exposure is "very limited" (President, CEO Bratt).

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical, with repeated probing on sustainability of China outperformance, tariff rule changes, raw-material spikes, and downside scenarios, including "if the strait doesn't open, there's more downside" (Colin Langan, Wells Fargo).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A, emphasizing limits on disclosure ("we don't disclose a breakdown") and uncertainty monitoring ("we follow this") while reiterating guidance (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Versus the prior quarter, more of the dialogue centered on geopolitical-driven LVP and commodities ("hostilities around the Persian Gulf") and less on year-end record performance framing; management maintained a similar confidence posture but with more caveats tied to evolving conditions (President, CEO Bratt).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The company highlighted a CEO-led CFO transition: Q4 2025 CFO was Fredrik Westin, while Q1 2026 introduced "our new CFO, Monika Grama" (President, CEO Bratt), and Q1 noted buybacks were paused during the restricted period (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Raw-material framing shifted from Q4’s expectation of "more around $30 million headwinds" for 2026 (Fredrik Westin) to Q1’s "around USD 90 million gross impact" for 2026 (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Tariff recovery commentary shifted from Q4’s "close to 100%" recovery in the quarter (President, CEO Bratt) to Q1’s "recovered approximately 70%" and references to delays from a new program (CFO Grama).

  • Q4 emphasized record full-year outcomes and a "clear path towards achieving our 10% adjusted operating margin target" (President, CEO Bratt), while Q1 closing remarks emphasized a "clear path towards achieving our 12% adjusted operating margin target" (President, CEO Bratt).

Risks and concerns

  • "Heightened geopolitical uncertainty from the hostilities around the Persian Gulf adds risk to energy markets, consumer confidence and overall industry volumes" (President, CEO Bratt), with mitigation described as scenario monitoring and readiness to adjust (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Raw-material inflation risk was tied to oil-linked inputs, with management citing possible impact to "textiles and plastics" and indirect exposure to "aluminum, helium and steel" (President, CEO Bratt), and mitigation via "productivity and cost reduction initiatives" plus customer compensation mechanisms (President, CEO Bratt).

  • Execution risks discussed included tariff recovery timing (CFO Grama) and working-capital volatility from end-of-quarter sales, which management said was temporary and expected to reverse (President, CEO Bratt; CFO Grama).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as ahead of internal expectations on sales and operations, with continued momentum in Asia driven by China and India outperformance, while keeping full-year 2026 guidance unchanged for flat organic sales, a 10.5% to 11% adjusted operating margin, and about $1.2 billion of operating cash flow. The discussion repeatedly returned to investor-sensitive swing factors—tariff recovery timing, the step-up in the gross raw-material headwind to about $90 million, and geopolitical risks around the Persian Gulf—while management pointed to structural cost savings, productivity initiatives, and customer compensation mechanisms as the main offsets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
